desc: LA's most spectacular insect. Metallic blue-black with orange wings. Hunts tarantulas — paralyzes them with one of the most painful stings in the insect world. Schmidt Pain Index 4/4.

fm.Color: Metallic blue-black body; vivid orange wings
fm.Sting: Schmidt Pain Index 4/4 — among most painful insect stings on Earth. Brief (3–5 min).
fm.Behavior: ♀ hunts tarantulas; paralyzes with sting; drags to burrow; lays egg on paralyzed spider

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Pompilidae.

desc: Second species of tarantula hawk in LA. Similar to Pepsis but slightly smaller. Same orange wings, excruciating sting.

fm.Color: Blue-black body; orange wings (darker than Pepsis)
fm.Sting: Schmidt Pain Index 4/4 — comparable to Pepsis
fm.Behavior: Same tarantula-hunting behavior as Pepsis

hp: Specialist tarantula hunter. Paralyzes tarantula, lays single egg. Orange wings warn of potent sting.

desc: The common yellowjacket of LA picnics. Ground-nesting. Scavenges meat and sweets. Aggressive defender. Causes most wasp stings.

fm.Color: Yellow and black banded; bright pattern
fm.Sting: Painful; repeated stinging; allergic reactions possible
fm.Behavior: Ground nest; scavenger; aggressive near food; peak aggression fall
fm.Habitat: Underground nests in gardens, parks, natural areas

hp: Social yellowjacket — scavenges protein (meat) and sugars. Preys on caterpillars, flies. Ground-nesting. Disrupts picnics.

desc: The common golden paper wasp of LA eaves and porches. Builds open-comb umbrella nest. Less aggressive than yellowjackets.

fm.Sting: Painful but less aggressive than Vespula
fm.Behavior: Open paper nest under eaves; predator of caterpillars; social

hp: Social paper nest builder — hunts insects for larvae. Vespidae.

desc: Introduced, now the most abundant paper wasp in urban LA, outcompeting native P. aurifer. More yellow than native species. Builds paper nests under eaves. More aggressive near food sources than native paper wasps. Rapidly became dominant since 1990s arrival.

fm.Color: Bright yellow and black; antennae orange-tipped
fm.Behavior: Nests in any cavity — walls, mailboxes, grills, patio umbrellas

hp: Social paper nest builder — hunts insects for larvae. Urban habitat.

desc: Black and yellow thread-waisted wasp that builds mud tube nests on walls. Stuffs paralyzed spiders inside for larvae.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; very thin waist
fm.Behavior: Builds mud tube nests; hunts spiders; stuffs into tubes; solitary
fm.Habitat: Buildings, bridges, walls, overhangs — any sheltered surface

hp: Mud nest builder — provisions with spiders/insects.

desc: Metallic blue-black. Does NOT build own nest — repurposes Sceliphron nests. Specializes in hunting black widows.

fm.Behavior: Repurposes mud dauber nests; specializes in hunting black widows and other cobweb spiders
fm.Habitat: Near existing mud dauber nests; urban structures

hp: Builds irregular cobwebs in dark sheltered spaces — garages, woodpiles, meter boxes. Sphecidae.

desc: Slender black wasp that builds parallel mud tubes resembling organ pipes. Provisions with spiders. Impressive architecture. Common on buildings.

fm.Behavior: Builds "organ pipe" mud tubes; fills with paralyzed spiders

hp: Indoor spider — beneficial pest control. Provisions mud cells with paralyzed spiders.

desc: Large, spectacular orange-and-black wasp. Digs burrows in sandy soil. Provisions with paralyzed katydids/grasshoppers.

fm.Color: Head/thorax black with golden pubescence; abdomen orange
fm.Sting: Can sting but non-aggressive; focused on digging
fm.Behavior: Digs burrows; hunts katydids/grasshoppers; paralyzes and drags to burrow

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Massive wasp (30-40mm) that hunts cicadas. Female catches cicada in flight, stings it, flies (barely — heavy load) to underground burrow. Each cell gets 1-3 cicadas. Despite terrifying size, gentle — males lack stingers, females rarely sting unless handled.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal bands; amber wings
fm.Sting: Can sting but rarely does; focused on cicadas
fm.Behavior: ♀ hunts cicadas; catches in flight; burrows in soil
fm.Habitat: Sandy soil near areas with cicadas

hp: Cicada hunter — catches cicadas in flight for larval provisions.

desc: Not an ant — a wingless female wasp. Dense orange velvet pile. Parasitizes ground-nesting bee/wasp larvae. Painful sting.

fm.Color: ♀: wingless; black with dense orange-red velvet pile. ♂: winged; less colorful.
fm.Sting: ♀: very painful (Schmidt 3/4); "cow killer" nickname (can't actually kill cows)
fm.Behavior: ♀ enters host wasp/bee nests; lays eggs on host larvae
fm.Season: May–Oct; ♀ wander ground seeking host nests

hp: Parasitoid of solitary bee/wasp nests. Ant associate.

desc: Extraordinary — dense white velvet pile makes it look like a thistledown seed blowing across desert sand. Unique camouflage.

fm.Color: ♀: entirely white/cream dense velvet pile; resembles thistledown
fm.Behavior: ♀ walks on hot desert sand; white pile reflects heat; parasitizes bees
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy washes — NE LA County

hp: On thistles (Cirsium/Carduus) — Asteraceae.

desc: Stocky wasp that burrows in sand. Provisions nest with paralyzed flies. Rapid circular flights around nesting area.

fm.Behavior: Mass nesting in sand; provisions with flies; rapid buzzy flight
fm.Habitat: Sandy beaches, sandy soil, sand dunes

hp: Ground-nesting in sand. Provisions burrow with paralyzed insect prey for larvae. Crabronidae.

desc: Not a true hornet (actually a yellowjacket). Black with white face markings. Builds large (football-sized) enclosed paper nests in trees. Aggressively defends nest — can sting repeatedly. Beneficial predator of many pest insects. Workers feed on flower nectar.

fm.Color: Black with white face and body markings
fm.Sting: Aggressive near nest; painful; repeated stinging
fm.Behavior: Large enclosed paper nest in trees/structures; social
fm.Habitat: Trees, shrubs, building overhangs — montane areas more common

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Large species.

desc: European hornet — the only true hornet (genus Vespa) in western NA. Large brown-and-yellow. Much larger than yellowjackets. Rare in LA County. Nests in cavities.

fm.Behavior: Paper nest in tree cavities; hunts other insects

hp: Social scavenger and predator.

desc: Tiny wasp in obligate mutualism with figs. Pollinates fig flowers internally. Every fig you eat was pollinated by a fig wasp.

fm.Behavior: ♀ enters fig through ostiole; pollinates; lays eggs; dies inside fig

hp: Pollinator — visits flowers for nectar and pollen. On Ficus (fig).

desc: Tiny parasitoid wasp. Larvae develop inside hornworm caterpillars, emerging as white cocoon clusters on caterpillar's back.

fm.Behavior: ♀ lays eggs inside caterpillar; larvae feed internally; emerge as white cocoons on host back

hp: Hunts caterpillars for larval provisions. Important natural pest control. Preys on caterpillar. Parasitoid. Tiny parasitoid wasp.

desc: Spectacular metallic blue-black wasp that hunts crickets. Enters cricket burrows, stings occupant, drags paralyzed cricket to own nest. Brilliant blue color unmistakable. Large (25mm). One of the most visually striking wasps in LA County.

fm.Sting: Can sting but focused on crickets
fm.Behavior: Hunts crickets/katydids; drags to burrow in soil

hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. Ant associate.

desc: Four-banded stink bug hunter — stocky black-and-yellow crabronid that specifically hunts stink bugs. Stings, paralyzes, and carries prey to ground nest. Nests in sandy soil.

fm.Behavior: Hunts stink bugs; ground nester

hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Stings, paralyzes, and carries prey to ground nest.

desc: Dark paper wasp with reddish-brown markings and distinctive ringed abdominal pattern. Builds open-face umbrella nests under protected overhangs. More robust than P. aurifer. Semi-social with dominance hierarchy among foundresses. Less common than P. dominula in urban LA.

fm.Behavior: Social; paper nest under overhangs

hp: Social paper nest builder — hunts insects for larvae. Urban habitat.

desc: Large (25mm), impressive sawfly with distinctive clubbed antennae. Larvae on willows and elms. Despite wasp-like appearance, sawflies are primitive Hymenoptera — larvae are plant-feeding caterpillar-mimics with six or more pairs of prolegs. Does not sting.

fm.Color: Dark metallic blue-black; yellow lateral spots
fm.Behavior: Adults on flowers; larvae on willow/elm leaves

hp: Hunts caterpillars for larval provisions. Important natural pest control. Associated with Willow.

desc: American pelecinid — unmistakable — female has an extraordinarily elongated abdomen (50-60mm, longer than body) used to reach June beetle grubs underground in soil. The longest abdomen-to-body ratio of any NA insect. Males extremely rare — reproduction mostly parthenogenetic.

fm.Size: ♀: up to 3 in total (extremely elongated abdomen)
fm.Behavior: ♀ probes soil with long abdomen to reach beetle grubs; parasitoid

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Preys on insects.

desc: Builds large paper nests in trees and on buildings (unlike V. pensylvanica which nests underground). Can be aggressive defending nest. Native.

fm.Color: Black with yellow markings (not white like D. maculata)
fm.Behavior: Aerial paper nest in trees; social

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Vespidae.

desc: Small wasp with flag-like abdomen held upright like a signal flag. Parasitoid of cockroach egg cases (oothecae) — female inserts single egg into roach egg case. Larva consumes all cockroach eggs inside. Highly beneficial in homes. Pantropical, common indoors.

fm.Color: Black; small; abdomen on high petiole like a flag
fm.Behavior: Seeks cockroach egg cases; lays egg inside; larva destroys eggs; beneficial
fm.Habitat: Urban, synanthropic — wherever cockroaches are found

hp: Predator or parasitoid of cockroaches.

desc: Large (20mm) black mason wasp with white abdominal band. Nests in pre-existing cavities — old mud dauber nests, hollow stems, nail holes. Hunts caterpillars to provision cells. Named for four teeth on mandibles. Gentle — rarely stings. Beneficial.

fm.Color: Black with single white band on first abdominal segment
fm.Behavior: Nests in beetle borings, hollow stems, bee hotels; hunts caterpillars

hp: Caterpillar predator — hunts moth and butterfly larvae. Preys on caterpillar. Nests in pre-existing cavities — old mud dauber.

desc: Introduced from Europe. Often nests in wall voids and structures (unlike native V. pensylvanica which prefers underground). More aggressive than native yellowjackets around food sources. Scavenger at picnics and trash. Capable of repeated stinging.

fm.Color: Yellow and black; very similar to V. pensylvanica but thicker face marks
fm.Behavior: Nests in wall voids, attics, underground; scavenges extensively

hp: Social scavenger and predator. Associated with Ash.

desc: Slender, long-legged social wasp building small pendant paper nests (10-50 cells). Less aggressive than Polistes — rarely stings unless nest directly threatened. Yellow tarsi diagnostic. Hunts caterpillars for larvae. Native to western NA.

fm.Color: Dark brown with yellow markings; very long legs
fm.Behavior: Small pendant paper nest under eaves; long dangling legs in flight

hp: Caterpillar predator — hunts moth and butterfly larvae. Preys on caterpillar. Less aggressive than Polistes — rarely stings unless.

desc: Gold-marked digger wasp — dark thread-waisted wasp with distinctive golden spot on thorax. Hunts large caterpillars in chaparral. Provisions underground nest.

fm.Color: Black with golden dorsal thorax spot
fm.Sting: Can sting but focused on caterpillars
fm.Behavior: Hunts caterpillars; carries them in flight to burrow

hp: Hunts caterpillars for larval provisions. Important natural pest control. Preys on caterpillar. Provisions underground nest.

desc: Tiny wasp that induces spectacular "oak apple" galls on oak trees — golf-ball sized or larger. Complex lifecycle.

fm.Behavior: ♀ injects egg + chemicals into oak bud; tree grows gall around larva; alternating sexual/asexual generations
fm.Season: Galls visible year-round; adults emerge spring

hp: Gall-inducer on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Species-specific gall form. Tiny. Oak Gall.

desc: One of the most abundant paper wasps after the introduced European P. dominula. Small open-comb nests under eaves. Less aggressive than yellowjackets.

fm.Behavior: Open paper nest under eaves

hp: Social paper nest builder — hunts insects for larvae. Introduced species.

desc: Toltec scoliid — large hairy wasp. Female digs to find scarab grubs (white grubs), stings and paralyzes them, and lays egg on host. Beneficial — controls lawn grubs.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal spots; hairy
fm.Behavior: Digs to parasitize scarab grubs; visits flowers

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Scoliidae.

desc: Orange-red haired wingless female wasp. Males are winged but rarely seen. Parasitizes ground-nesting bees and wasps. Sting reportedly excruciating.

fm.Behavior: ♀ walks ground seeking host nests

hp: Velvet ant — parasitizes ground-nesting bee/wasp nests. Family Mutillidae. Parasitizes ground-nesting bees and wasps.

desc: The most ecologically destructive invasive ant in SoCal. Forms supercolonies stretching hundreds of miles. Displaces native ants. Dominant in urban habitats.

fm.Behavior: Trails; tends aphids; displaces natives

hp: Most ecologically destructive invasive ant in SoCal (Holway et al. 2002, Ann Rev Ecol Syst). Supercolonial. Displaces 90%+ native ants.

desc: Velvety tree ant — large conspicuous ant nesting in tree cavities and under bark. Trails on tree trunks. Emits strong acrid coconut-like odor when disturbed. Native.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; acrid coconut odor when disturbed

hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Under bark. Large species.

desc: Large red ant building crater-shaped nests with cleared vegetation disc. Extremely painful sting — among the most painful of any ant. Seed harvester.

fm.Color: Red; crater nest with cleared disc

hp: Indoor trailing ant — nuisance pest. Ant associate.

desc: Native fire ant. Stings painfully in swarming defense. Nests in soil mounds. Less aggressive than imported red fire ant but still formidable.
hp: Native fire ant. Soil mounds. Painful sting. Important seed disperser and soil engineer.

desc: Tiny invasive ant from South America. Invades structures in massive numbers. Attracted to sweet liquids. Can be extremely abundant. Difficult to control.
hp: Attracted to electrical equipment.

desc: Small solitary wasp nesting in pre-existing holes including bee hotels. Provisions with moth larvae. Black with pale markings. Garden beneficial.

fm.Color: Black with pale bands; uses bee hotels

hp: Hidalgo mason wasp — provisions cavity nest with paralyzed moth caterpillars. Mud-partitioned cells. Beneficial garden predator.

desc: Paper wasp — small docile paper wasp building small open-comb nests. Less aggressive than P. dominula. Important caterpillar predator. Beneficial garden wasp.
hp: Hunts caterpillars for larval provisions. Important natural pest control. Preys on caterpillar. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Large dark spider wasp with orange wings. Hunts tarantulas. Famously painful sting (Schmidt pain scale 4/4). Third Pepsis species in the LA basin.

fm.Warning: Among the most painful stings of any insect

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Scale insect associate.

desc: Tiny ant that smells like coconut or blue cheese when crushed. The most common kitchen-invading ant. Trail-following forager. Nests in walls and under objects.

fm.Behavior: Kitchen trails; coconut odor

hp: Indoor trailing ant — nuisance pest. Ant associate.

desc: Native paper wasp of arid habitats. Smaller and more slender than P. dominula. Builds open paper nests under eaves and in shrubs.
hp: Social paper nest builder — hunts insects for larvae. Family Vespidae. Native paper wasp of arid habitats. Smaller and more slender.

desc: Thread-waisted sand wasp — large metallic blue-black wasp hunting katydids. Digs burrow in sandy soil, provisions with paralyzed katydids. Desert and arid scrub.
hp: Burrowing ambush predator. Desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Meadow mason wasp — small solitary vespid nesting in pre-existing holes. Provisions with caterpillars. Common in gardens.
hp: Ecological role from description context

desc: Wingless female wasp covered in dense orange-red velvet pile. Males winged. Extremely painful sting — 'cow killer.' Parasitizes ground-nesting bees.
hp: Velvet ant — parasitizes ground-nesting bee/wasp nests. Family Mutillidae. ' Parasitizes ground-nesting bees.

desc: Elegant grass-carrier wasp — slender black wasp that stuffs grass and plant material into cavities for nesting. Provisions with tree crickets. Common in bee hotels.

fm.Color: Black; stuffs grass into nest cavities

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland

desc: Cross-marked potter wasp — builds beautiful miniature clay pot nests attached to twigs. Each pot provisioned with paralyzed caterpillars. Remarkable mud architecture.

fm.Behavior: Builds exquisite mud pot nests on twigs

hp: Hunts caterpillars for larval provisions. Preys on caterpillar.

desc: Builds remarkable mud pot nests attached to twigs. Each pot provisioned with paralyzed caterpillars. Master potter.
hp: Hunts caterpillars for larval provisions. Preys on caterpillar. Boll's.

desc: Endemic California yellowjacket. Less aggressive than German yellowjacket. Nests in ground cavities. Important predator of pest caterpillars.
hp: Caterpillar predator — hunts moth and butterfly larvae. Preys on caterpillar. Endemic California yellowjacket. Less aggressive than German.

desc: Highly invasive ant with extremely painful sting. Originally from South America. Massive colonies. Spreading into SoCal. Medical and agricultural threat.

fm.Warning: Highly invasive — very painful sting — spreading in SoCal

hp: Red imported fire ant — mass-stinging defense causes anaphylaxis risk. $6B+ annual US damage (Lard et al. 2006). Not established in LA but under surveillance.

desc: Medium wasp with humped thorax. Hunts small bees. Provisions underground burrow. Colonial nester.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; humped; hunts bees

hp: Ecological role from description context. Crabronidae.

desc: Large hairy wasp hunting scarab beetle grubs underground. Important biocontrol of white grubs. Visits flowers.
hp: Introduced for biological pest control. Ant associate.

desc: Variable facial patterns. Recognizes nestmate faces individually — advanced social cognition.

fm.Color: Dark brown; variable facial markings (individual recognition)
fm.Behavior: Social; facial recognition of nestmates (rare in insects)

hp: Social paper nest builder — hunts insects for larvae. Vespidae.

desc: Architect spider wasp — metallic blue pompilid that builds mud cells inside existing cavities. Hunts spiders, paralyzes them, stuffs them into mud cells with one egg each. The mud architecture gives the species its name. On building walls and wood fences. Iridescent blue-black.

fm.Behavior: Builds mud cells in cavities; hunts spiders; common in bee hotels
fm.Habitat: Cavities — bee hotels, hollow stems, walls

hp: Spider-hunting pompilid — paralyzes spider with venom, provisions nest for single larva. Spider associate.

desc: One of the largest NA wasps (25-35mm). Entirely black. Hunts katydids and large grasshoppers — carries paralyzed prey in flight to underground burrow. Impressive size and dramatic hunting behavior. Important pollinator of milkweed. Gentle despite intimidating size.

fm.Behavior: Digs deep burrows; provisions with paralyzed Orthoptera

hp: Milkweed; predatory; grass; pollinator. Associated with Narrow-leaf Milkweed.

desc: Western yellow-spotted mud dauber — western subspecies with more extensive yellow markings than eastern form. Builds characteristic parallel mud tubes (organ pipe nest) in sheltered locations. Each cell provisioned with paralyzed spiders. Gentle — rarely stings. Beneficial spider predator.

fm.Color: Black with yellow legs and markings
fm.Behavior: Builds mud tubes on walls; provisions with spiders

hp: Spider-hunting pompilid — paralyzes spider with venom, provisions nest for single larva. Provisions mud cells with paralyzed spiders.

desc: Satan velvet ant — large (15-20mm) black-and-orange mutillid with extraordinarily painful sting — rated among the most painful insect stings in North America. Female wingless and densely covered in velvet-like orange setae. Males winged, rarely seen. Parasitizes ground-nesting bees and wasps. Desert and chaparral.

fm.Color: Black with orange-red velvet pile; robust
fm.Behavior: ♀ walks ground seeking host nests; ♂ winged, smaller

hp: Velvet ant — parasitizes ground-nesting bee/wasp nests.

desc: The only true hornet (genus Vespa) established in North America. Much larger than yellowjackets. Brown and yellow. Builds paper nests in tree hollows and wall voids. Nocturnal — attracted to lights. Sting painful but not more dangerous than yellowjacket sting.

fm.Color: Brown and yellow; much larger than yellowjackets
fm.Behavior: Paper nest in tree hollows; hunts other insects; attracted to lights at night

hp: Only true hornet in North America. Colony predator — hunts other insects. European introduction.

desc: Small, dark potter wasp that nests in small holes and keyholes. Has caused aviation incidents by blocking pitot tubes.

fm.Behavior: Nests in small cavities including keyholes, instrument ports

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Vespidae.

desc: California weevil wasp — ground-nesting wasp that exclusively hunts weevils and small beetles. Yellow-and-black. Used by entomologists as biological detection tool — wasps collecting beetles near their nests reveal which borer species are present in the area.

fm.Behavior: Hunts weevils and small beetles; ground nester in hard-packed soil

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Crabronidae.

desc: Golden fire ant — native fire ant, smaller colonies and less aggressive than the infamous imported red fire ant (S. invicta). Declining as Argentine ants displace it from many habitats. Important native seed disperser and soil aerator. Stings mild compared to imported species.

fm.Behavior: Stings in defense; declining from Argentine ant competition

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Formicidae.

desc: Native desert ant with distinctive crater-shaped nest entrance. Bicolored — red head, dark gaster. Fast runner.

fm.Colony: Crater-shaped nest entrance in sandy soil (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Extremely fast runner; forages at high temperatures
fm.Habitat: Desert, sandy soil, hot open areas

hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Desert specialist. Fast-moving.

desc: Large native carpenter ant. Bicolored. Nests in dead wood but does NOT eat wood (unlike termites). Nocturnal forager.

fm.Colony: In dead wood, tree cavities; does not eat wood
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal foraging trails; large workers have powerful mandibles
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, urban with dead wood

hp: Ecological role from description context

desc: Female has ovipositor two to three times body length. Drills through solid wood to parasitize horntail larvae deep inside tree trunks. Can detect host vibrations through wood. Despite fearsome appearance, cannot sting humans — ovipositor is too flexible.

fm.Description: Female has ovipositor longer than body — drills through wood to parasitize horntail larvae

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Parasitoid.

desc: Blue-winged scoliid — black wasp with iridescent blue wings and two orange abdominal spots. Female digs into turf to find and parasitize Japanese beetle and other scarab grubs. Important biocontrol agent of turf-destroying white grubs. Males patrol low over lawns.

fm.Description: Black wasp with blue wings and orange abdominal spots

hp: Biocontrol agent; parasitoid of bees.

desc: Pigeon horntail — large (35mm) wood wasp (sawfly). Female drills into hardwoods with stout ovipositor, injecting eggs along with symbiotic fungus that weakens wood for larval feeding. Host of giant ichneumon wasps (Megarhyssa). Impressive size — alarming but harmless.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Ant associate.

desc: Brown-bodied paper wasp building small open-face nests under eaves and overhangs. Less aggressive than yellowjackets — stings only when nest directly threatened. Hunts caterpillars to feed larvae. Foundress queens start nests alone in spring.
hp: Caterpillar predator — hunts moth and butterfly larvae. Preys on caterpillar. Less aggressive than yellowjackets — stings only when.

desc: Mourning velvety tree ant — close relative of L. occidentale. Found in mountain areas on oaks and conifers. Strong coconut-like odor when disturbed. Trails on trunks.

fm.Habitat: Found in mountain areas on oaks and conifers

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Velvety.

desc: Large burrowing wasp of sandy beaches and desert washes. Provisions nest with flies caught in aerial pursuit. Unlike most wasps that mass-provision, Bembix feeds larvae progressively — mother returns daily with fresh prey. Extremely fast, agile flier.

fm.Habitat: Burrowing wasp of sandy beaches and washes

hp: Ground-nesting in sand. Provisions burrow with paralyzed insect prey for larvae. Associated with Ash.

desc: Supercolonial ant forming vast interconnected networks spanning multiple nests. Tends honeydew-producing aphids and scales — exacerbating crop pest problems. Spreading through southern California. Originally from western Asia. Unicolonial — no aggression between nests.
hp: Invasive introduction — ecological impact on native communities

desc: Red ant building distinctive gravel-topped mound nests with large cleared areas around entrance. Extremely painful sting — among the most painful of any NA ant. Important seed disperser in desert ecosystems. Harvests seeds stored in underground granaries.

fm.Sting: Very painful — rated 3 on Schmidt scale

hp: Seed harvester — collects and stores seeds in underground granary. Important seed disperser. Desert specialist.

desc: Western thatching ant — builds large thatch mounds (up to 2 ft tall) from plant debris in mountain meadows and oak woodland. Sprays concentrated formic acid as chemical defense — can strip enamel. Tends aphid colonies for honeydew. Important soil architects. Colonial.
hp: Sap feeder on oaks. Ant-tended. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Slender wasp hunting caterpillars. Digs burrow, stings and buries prey for larva. Uses pebble tool to tamp soil.

fm.Prey: Caterpillars — paralyzed and buried for larvae
fm.Behavior: Uses pebble as tool to tamp soil over burrow

hp: Hunts caterpillars for larval provisions. Important natural pest control. Preys on caterpillar. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Metallic blue pompilid that hunts wolf spiders and other ground spiders. Paralyzes prey with sting, drags to prepared burrow. Each cell provisioned with one spider for a single larva. Fast, nervous movements — runs constantly with flicking wings.
hp: Ground-running nocturnal hunter.

desc: Golden cuckoo wasp — brilliant metallic green-blue chrysidid with ruby-red abdomen. Cleptoparasite of mud dauber wasp nests. When discovered by host, rolls into an armored ball — thick exoskeleton protects from stings. Among the most beautiful insects in LA County.
hp: Mud nest builder — provisions cells with paralyzed prey (spiders or caterpillars).

desc: Spring tiphiid — black wasp that digs underground to parasitize white grubs (scarab beetle larvae). Female locates grub by burrowing, stings it to paralyze, lays egg on body surface. Important biological control of lawn-damaging Japanese beetle and masked chafer grubs.

fm.Nesting: Digs to parasitize white grubs underground
fm.Prey: Scarab larvae (Japanese beetle, June beetle grubs)

hp: Biocontrol agent; parasitoid of bees.

desc: Toltec scoliid — large hairy wasp that digs into soil to locate and parasitize scarab grubs underground. Female stings grub to paralyze it, lays egg on body. Yellow abdominal bands. Males smaller, patrol flowers. Important natural control of white grubs in turf.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal bands; hairy
fm.Nesting: Digs into soil for scarab grubs

hp: Predatory or parasitoid wasp. Important in natural pest control and ecosystem function.

desc: California torymid — tiny metallic green parasitoid wasp. Ovipositor drills into plant galls to parasitize gall-maker larvae inside. Part of the extremely diverse Torymidae — each gall wasp species has its own suite of torymid parasitoids. Important biological control agent.

fm.Role: Parasitoid of gall-forming insects on oaks

hp: Biocontrol agent; parasitoid of wasps.

desc: Dark rover ant — tiny fast-running ant with distinctive erratic, darting movement. Indoor nuisance pest forming trailing lines in kitchens and bathrooms. Multiple queens per colony (polygynous). Originally tropical — now widespread in warm buildings. Does not sting or bite meaningfully.

fm.Behavior: Erratic zigzag movement — "crazy ant"

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Formicidae.

desc: Small ant that raises heart-shaped abdomen (shaped like a spade) up over thorax when alarmed — a diagnostic 'acrobatic' posture. Tree-dwelling, nesting in dead branches and under bark. Tends honeydew insects. Bites but does not sting effectively.

fm.Defense: Raises abdomen over body when alarmed

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. On tree bark

desc: LA's most common structural termite. Unlike subterranean termites, needs no soil contact — lives entirely within dry wood. Winged reproductives swarm September-November. Produces diagnostic hexagonal fecal pellets. Causes billions in damage across SoCal.

fm.Damage: Eats wood; distinctive hexagonal fecal pellets

hp: Wood consumer — structural pest.

desc: LA's most common native termite. Builds mud tubes from soil to wood, maintaining moisture connection. Colonies of hundreds of thousands. Causes significant structural damage. Distinguished from drywood termites by soil-contact requirement and tube construction.

fm.Damage: Mud shelter tubes diagnostic; requires soil moisture

hp: Mud nest builder — provisions with spiders/insects. Sandy habitat nester.

desc: Small dark ant nesting under sidewalks and pavement cracks. Characteristic sand mounds at pavement joints. Aggressive toward other ant colonies — sidewalk 'ant wars' in spring.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; grooved head and thorax
fm.Habitat: Under pavement, sidewalk cracks — sand mounds at joints
fm.Behavior: Mass 'ant wars' between colonies on warm spring days

hp: Ground-nesting in sand. Provisions burrow with paralyzed insect prey for larvae. In ant nests.
